data.title: FuelCell Energy: Not A Lot Of Faith This Can Be A Winner
data.description:
Investment Thesis

FuelCell Energy (FCEL) I remember being one of the supposed big ones in the green energy wave that occurred a few years ago. Its technology is fascinating and highly scalable, but the production isn't there and neither are the margins right now.

Weighing this with insiders both selling and the company still diluting massive amounts of shares, likely even more so now after the stock rose so sharply in October of this year. I think you’d be better off staying away from the stock for the moment, which is why I’m rating it a sell.

Stock Chart 5-Year Performance

For the price comparison I’ve decided to look at Bloom Energy (BE), Plug Power (PLUG), Ballard Power Systems (BLDP) and Invesco WinderHill Clean Energy ETF (PBW) as they offer similar exposure. All are developing some form of fuel cell but as the chart shows there has been but one winner here.

Bloom Energy has been on a massive run this year as it continues to ramp up production and managed to get both gross, operating and net margins positive on a TTM basis. FCEL, which is today's stock to discuss, is still very far from this goal.

On a YTD basis FCEL is down nearly 30% but did surge from below $4 to nearly $12 per share in a matter of 2 months or so. The industry does have a lot of potential as a viable option for scaling power generation whilst at the same time also reducing the amount of emissions and collecting credits for this. A few years ago it would have been very tough to choose a winner which is why I’ve mostly stayed out of this industry all together. But since some of the stocks have been on such a run this year I figured I’d give it a look. Despite the run, YTD Bloom Energy still looks to be by far the best play here because of its already scaled production and much clearer path towards higher margins, compared to both FCEL and PLUG.

Operations Overview

FCEL I remember being a very talked about company a few years ago as the technology it had seemed revolutionary for the green energy wave as it could help in capturing more carbon emissions and make gas generated electricity far more environmentally friendly. It has since transformed into portraying itself more as a play on data centers and being able to scale with the energy demand that these have.

In the core FCEL is an electrochemical technology company selling a platform of sorts, a platform which basically takes the natural gas inputs, and transforms that into electricity and then is able to capture a large portion of the carbon emissions from this process. Because of the relatively small size and in theory high scalability FCEL could rapidly be deployed along sites where companies are building data centers.

Above is an image depicting the % of sales each segment has contributed over the past 5 years, measured for the Q3 for all. If I would be more granular, the chart would be slightly less dramatic, but this is the reality with FCEL, revenue streams differ heavily from year to year and quarter to quarter. FCEL reports 4 different revenue lines: Product, Service, Generation and Advanced Technology. Out of these the most important for the future is without a doubt “Product” as it relates to the sale of its oxide fuel cells. The uses are not just carbon capture but could be for hydrogen generation and overall power generation as well. The diversified use case is perhaps an understated advantage for FCEL.

As for the remaining three segments, Service relates to revenues generated from mult-year contracts where FCEL basically maintains the performance of the already installed fuel cells, along with monitoring services. These revenues tend to be more stable as they are recurring.

The third segment is Generation which as the name suggests relates to electricity sales that FCEL itself generated as it owns an asset base of power generation. Revenues come on a metered output and again can be relatively stable.

Lastly we have the Advanced Technologies segment which instead revolves around R&D and copiloting fuel cell installations along with general carbon capture and hydrogen projects. Importantly is that a large portion of these revenues are funded by grants. From agreements with companies like ExxonMobil (XOM) for example.

Above I’ve made a graph showing the gross margins for each of the revenue lines, also during the same period as the table before. What immediately jumps out is the steady decline of both product and advanced technologies margins. But on the contrary Generation is posting a strong uptrend, going from negative 66.7% in Q3 2022 to now negative 24% now. Importantly we’d want to see the first segment improve much faster seeing as that will be the main revenue stream over the long-term most likely.

Measured on a 9 month basis for 2025 and 2024 the results actually look a lot better with the combined revenue amount growing 64.22% YoY, most of that coming from increased revenues in Product sales. Again, besides just growth in revenues, FCEL has also delivered a strong gross margin improvement going from negative 39.8% to now negative 19.2% YTD. Most of the cost savings came from the Generation segment. The cited reason in the 10Q is lower costs relating to the Toyota Project.

On the topic of margins, operating margins are deeply negative sitting at (-98%) now but have improved from (-187%) during the first nine months of 2024. I should say that I am using normalized operating losses, meaning I don’t account for the impairment charges the company incurred during 2025 as I believe it does not represent the core operations. The impairment charge in question was described to be about inventory and goodwill along with certain investments the company has previously made.

Impairment expense of $64.5 million for the three months ended July 31, 2025 related to the Company's prior investments in solid oxide technology, including related goodwill and in-process research and development (“IPR&D”) intangible assets, property, plant and equipment and solid oxide inventory. Of the $64.5 million, approximately $42.1 million was related to property, plant and equipment, approximately $9.0 million was related to inventory, approximately $9.3 million was related to IPR&D intangible assets, and approximately $4.1 million was related to goodwill.

During the past few years the cost of SG&A has come down as well quite significantly and is now more in line with R&D expenses as a % of the overall OPEX. This is a positive sign seeing as the company has also grown its headcount from 316 in 2020 to as the last report 584. More normalized OPEX lines from here on will make valuing the stock a lot easier.

Weak Margins & High Dilution

The path to profitability is on every investor's mind right now in FCEL. In the past few years the company has done a solid job of raising both gross and operating margins as I’ve discussed earlier here. The issue seems to stem more now from actually scaling and being able to deliver more fuel cells.

Generation is by far the largest segment right now in terms of backlogs. Quite disappointingly is the fact that the product segment is barely seeing any rise in backlogs even as the company tries to expand in data centers. This expansion is not a new thing, so some effect should have been seen if this was an appealing proposal for companies. The lack thereof is a worrying sign.

Currently I don't see a viable path to profitability for the company. It has continued to underperform the market expectations and even if revenues have risen FCEL continues to lack the capability of bringing costs down. On one hand you have quite constrained growth in the OPEX lines, but since this is a company planning to scale off of fuel cell sales and that cost item is still largely in the negative, -11.85% last quarter and with years or barely any sales at all I think a lot of pessimism here is warranted.

Because of the lack of profitability so far the company continues to resort to equity financing as its main source of capital to help sustain operations. With a current cash pile of $174 million, growing from $148 million last year FCEL should be able to bear another 5 - 6 quarters of normalized OPEX. Just YoY the diluted share number grew by 45.7%, going from 16.772 million to 24.441 million. Seeing as the last report ended on July 31 and the price surged in the months after that I’d expect the next report from the company to show a serious amount of further dilution as well. We’ll know more on December 18th when the next report is set to come.

The supposed 30% annual operating expense cuts I don't see as realistic, from what exact line of expense would that come from? Is the company going to fire a lot of people or cut back on R&D spending and lose out on their competitive edge? Neither is good so this statement from management unfortunately doesn't hold a lot of weight in my opinion. Unless they can get to the core issue which is the costs of essentially all their revenue items then there isn’t much of a positive case here to be made.

The Tough Reality

The tough reality is that FCEL is nowhere close to profitability today than it was a few years ago. The difference instead has a massive amount of dilution and a backlog which seems to be depleting and worked through, but not actually being able to translate into even gross profits for the business.

On a Price/Sales or Price/Book metric the stock doesn't look overvalued and to some extent would even suggest an undervaluation based on the book value. However, because of the restructuring plan the company announced last year, which so far seems to have resulted in the asset base falling, the valuation that seems cheap is hiding the fact that asset values is likely to decrease further, causing the multiple to rise.

Share-based compensation exists in almost every company, but $8.6 million YTD seems excessive seeing as the business is not even producing a gross profit on a reliable basis yet. It has slowed down YoY but without it operating cash flows would have been about 8 - 10% higher. This isn’t the time to be rewarding shares when the company is still not performing.

Based on some recent insider reports it doesn't seem that management either has a lot of value in holding shares. The CFO for instance has been selling almost as soon as receiving shares, holding about 10,138 shares right now. He received about 1,167 worth of shares and sold twice for a combined total of 688 shares at an average price of $8.87.

Weighing all of the negatives like disappointing overall margin development the past 5 years, massive share dilution and a depleting backlog which isn't growing, the stock does fit the criteria of being a sell.

The Full Picture

The fact of the matter is this, FuelCell has a very solid product but it has been unable to scale this over the past 5 years and is now still struggling with raising margins. There have been some improvements in certain revenue types, but overall it’s still deeply negative.

It seems that larger companies like Bloom Energy will have a much easier time outpacing and holding onto market share as opposed to FCEL. Its cash pile is larger and margins are positive as well.

I went into researching this stock thinking the market must have gotten the wrong idea here, but I have to agree and say that FCEL is a sell when combining all of the negatives I’ve pointed out here so far.

data.title: ECB Preview: Driving Home For Christmas
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

When the European Central Bank meets again next week, some will get a first idea of how their family Christmas dinner might look the week after: stimulating – at times controversial – conversations without any escalating disputes. Like gifts under the Christmas tree, the latest round of ECB projections will be awaited with high expectations by all ECB members. Some will be happier than others; some might even ignore them.

Developments since last meeting call for keeping rates on hold

Let’s leave the Christmas parallels aside for a moment. Since the ECB’s October meeting, the incoming data has done very little to justify any rate change on 18 December. While third quarter GDP growth was clearly stronger than the ECB expected, sentiment indicators also point to continued resilience. At the same time, the fundamentals of the eurozone economy have not changed. US tariffs still weigh on exports, investment is being held back by uncertainty, and 2026 growth remains highly dependent on German fiscal stimulus. Inflation has come in slightly higher than previously expected, but the delayed implementation of the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS2) should push expected inflation by some 0.2 percentage points from 2027 to 2028.

As a result, there’s very little reason for the ECB to change its current monetary policy stance, once again confirming it's in a ‘good place’.

New round of projections unlikely to reflect Schnabel's upside risks to growth and inflation

A fresh round of ECB projections will be an important element next week. While ECB board member Isabel Schnabel said this week that, compared with September, the risks to growth and inflation are tilted to the upside, we doubt this will be reflected in ECB projections. To the contrary, it looks very unlikely that the central bank will change the longer-term growth trajectory of 0.3% quarter-on-quarter in 2026 and 2027. Regarding inflation, the 2026 forecast may be raised slightly (from 1.7% year-on-year in the September projections), while 2027 should be revised downward due to the delayed implementation of ETS2. However, with inflation forecasts for 2026 and 2027 clearly below 2%, it’s difficult to quantify upside risks.

In her Bloomberg interview this week, Schnabel unexpectedly gave the upcoming ECB debate a hawkish tilt. She said she agreed with market expectations that the next move would be a hike, without providing any indication of timing. This comment, however, will be hard to find in the new ECB projections. It also doesn’t echo the sentiment of the minutes of the October meeting, which clearly had a dovish, not a hawkish, bias. This suggests that views at the ECB are diverging, not converging, and that remaining on hold appears to be the best compromise. As a result, we expect ECB President Christine Lagarde to refrain from providing any forward guidance or risk assessment of the inflation outlook at next week’s meeting.

Our base case continues seeing the ECB on hold for foreseeable future

Looking beyond next week, we don’t think that Schnabel’s comments currently reflect the ECB’s majority view. With expected sub-2% inflation forecasts for the next three years, we see any following ECB rate change as a cut, not a hike, at least through late spring next year. After that, the window for rate cuts will likely close, and fiscal stimulus that meets supply side constraints could bring back inflationary pressures. But that is a 2027 story, rather than one for 2026.

Musical chairs is on again

Finally, something that’s probably (for now) more for the ‘rumours and gossips’ corner: Schnabel’s comment that she would stand ready as a potential successor to Lagarde has sparked the musical chairs debate. Many seem to have overlooked the European Treaties, which clearly state that Executive Board members, including the ECB president and vice-president, are appointed for a single, non-renewable term.

More than 20 years ago, a debate arose over whether then-vice president Christian Noyer could become ECB president instead of the designated Jean-Claude Trichet. Legal opinions differed, but a key difference between then and now is that Noyer did not serve a full eight-year term at the ECB. This is because Executive Board members were phased in when the ECB was established. At the current juncture, trying to bend any Treaty rules by arguing that there’s a difference between an ‘ordinary’ Executive Board member and the presidency, just to allow an Executive Board member to become president, looks highly unlikely. Not everything legally possible is politically desirable.

Just like your normal Christmas dinner

All in all, next week’s ECB meeting promises to have all the ingredients of a typical family gathering at Christmas: stimulating and at times controversial conversations, a few rumours and a bit of gossip, accompanied by vague talks about the future but no hard action.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
